result:
The retry/wait-and-resend logic lives in:

**`src/core/api/retry.ts`** — the `withRetry()` decorator (its inner `descriptor.value` async generator).

It wraps each provider's `createMessage` stream. On a `429` status or a `RetriableError` (the "overloaded/rejected" case), it computes a delay — from the `retry-after` / `x-ratelimit-reset` / `ratelimit-reset` header or `error.retryAfter`, otherwise exponential backoff (`baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`, capped at `maxDelay`) — then `await`s a `setTimeout` (line 81) before looping to retry, up to `maxRetries` (default 3).

The retry/wait-and-retry logic lives in:

**`src/core/api/retry.ts`** — the `withRetry()` decorator (its inner `descriptor.value` async generator).

It wraps a provider's `createMessage` stream and, on a `429`/`RetriableError` (or any error when `retryAllErrors` is set), waits via `await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, delay))` — using the `retry-after`/rate-limit-reset header if present, otherwise exponential backoff — then re-yields the request, up to `maxRetries` (default 3).

The retry logic lives in **`src/core/api/retry.ts`** — specifically the **`withRetry`** decorator (and its `RetriableError` companion class).

It wraps a provider's streaming method in a loop that catches errors, treats `status === 429` / `RetriableError` (and optionally all errors) as retriable, waits via `await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, delay))`, and retries. The delay comes from the `retry-after` / `x-ratelimit-reset` / `ratelimit-reset` headers, falling back to exponential backoff (`baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`, capped at `maxDelay`).

**File:** `src/core/api/retry.ts` — the `withRetry` decorator (its returned async-generator `descriptor.value`).

This is where it waits and retries. On a caught error it checks `error?.status === 429` or a `RetriableError` (the "overloaded/rejected" case), then sleeps via `await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, delay))` — using the `retry-after`/`x-ratelimit-reset`/`ratelimit-reset` header if present, otherwise exponential backoff (`baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`, capped at `maxDelay`) — before re-running the request, up to `maxRetries` (default 3). Provider `createMessage` methods are annotated with `@withRetry()`.

The retry logic lives in **`src/core/api/retry.ts`**, in the **`withRetry()`** decorator (it wraps each provider's streaming `createMessage` generator).

When the server is overloaded or rejects the request — detected via `error?.status === 429` or a thrown `RetriableError` — the wait-and-retry happens at line 81:

```ts
await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, delay))
```

The `delay` is taken from the response's `retry-after` / `x-ratelimit-reset` / `ratelimit-reset` header (or `error.retryAfter`), falling back to exponential backoff (`baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`, capped at `maxDelay`). It retries up to `maxRetries` (default 3) times before rethrowing.

The wait-and-retry happens in **`src/core/api/retry.ts`**, in the **`withRetry`** decorator (the generator returned by its `descriptor.value`).

It wraps each provider's streaming `createMessage`. On a catch, it treats `error.status === 429` or a `RetriableError` as overload/rejection, then computes a delay — honoring `retry-after` / `x-ratelimit-reset` / `ratelimit-reset` headers (or the error's `retryAfter`), falling back to exponential backoff (`baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`, capped at `maxDelay`) — and waits via `await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, delay))` before the next of `maxRetries` (default 3) attempts.

The retry/wait logic lives in:

**`src/core/api/retry.ts`** — the **`withRetry()`** decorator (a generator-wrapping method decorator).

When a request throws, it catches the error, treats `status === 429` or a `RetriableError` as retriable, computes a delay from rate-limit headers (`retry-after` / `x-ratelimit-reset` / `ratelimit-reset` / `error.retryAfter`) or falls back to exponential backoff, then `await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, delay))` before re-running the wrapped `createMessage` generator. The `RetriableError` class in the same file (defaults to `status = 429`) is how providers signal an overloaded/rejecting server.

The retry/wait-and-resend logic lives in:

**File:** `src/core/api/retry.ts`
**Symbol:** the `withRetry()` decorator (its async-generator wrapper), supported by the `RetriableError` class.

It wraps each provider's `createMessage` generator. On a `429` (rate-limit) or `RetriableError` (server overloaded/rejection), it computes a delay — from a `retry-after` / `ratelimit-reset` header if present, otherwise exponential backoff (`baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`, capped at `maxDelay`) — then `await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, delay))` (line 81) before retrying, up to `maxRetries` (default 3).

The retry/wait-and-retry logic lives in:

**`src/core/api/retry.ts`** — the `withRetry()` decorator (its inner `descriptor.value` async generator).

It wraps each provider's `createMessage` stream. On a catch, it treats `status === 429` or a `RetriableError` (also any error when `retryAllErrors` is set) as retriable, computes a delay from the `retry-after` / `x-ratelimit-reset` / `ratelimit-reset` headers (or exponential backoff via `baseDelay * 2 ** attempt`), then `await new Promise(setTimeout)` before looping to retry — up to `maxRetries` (default 3).

The deduplication of concurrent identical network requests is in:

**`src/core/controller/models/refreshOpenRouterModels.ts`** — the module-level `pendingRefresh` promise guard inside the `refreshOpenRouterModels()` function.

If a fetch is already in flight, the function returns the existing `pendingRefresh` promise instead of starting a second request (lines 80–111); the `finally` block clears it once the fetch settles. The same pattern is repeated in the sibling `refresh*Models.ts` files (e.g. `refreshGroqModels.ts`, `refreshBasetenModels.ts`, `refreshVercelAiGatewayModels.ts`).

**`cli/src/utils/openrouter-models.ts`** — function **`fetchOpenRouterModels()`**, via the module-level **`fetchPromise`** guard.

If a fetch is already in progress, concurrent callers return the same `fetchPromise` instead of starting a new request (lines 29–30); it's set before the network call and cleared in `finally` (line 52). The `cachedModels` variable handles dedup *after* completion, but the in-flight coalescing — stopping a second simultaneous fire — is `fetchPromise`.

The guard lives in **`src/services/banner/BannerService.ts`**, in the **`ensureFreshCache()`** method (with the `private fetchPromise: Promise<Banner[]> | null` field).

It coalesces concurrent fetches by checking `!this.fetchPromise` before starting a new one — while a fetch is in flight, `fetchPromise` is non-null, so any further call skips firing a second request. It's reset to `null` in the `.finally()` once the in-flight fetch settles:

```ts
const shouldFetch =
    now >= this.backoffUntil &&
    now - this.lastFetchTime >= cacheDurationMs &&
    !this.fetchPromise &&          // ← in-flight dedup guard
    !this.authFetchPending

if (shouldFetch) {
    this.fetchPromise = this.doFetch()
    this.fetchPromise.finally(() => { this.fetchPromise = null })
}
```

The streamed assistant text is parsed into structured tool calls (tool blocks with parameters) here:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts` → `parseAssistantMessageV2()`**

It scans the raw streamed string for `<tool_name>...</tool_name>` blocks and their `<param>` tags, emitting `AssistantMessageContent[]` (interleaved text and `ToolUse` objects) that the executor then acts on. (This is the XML-tool path; providers using OpenAI's Responses API use native tool calls instead.)

The codebase guards against this in a few spots using the same "store the in-flight promise, return it if one already exists" pattern. The clearest, most explicit one:

**`src/services/auth/AuthService.ts`** — field `_refreshPromise` (`Promise<string | undefined> | null`). In `getAuthToken()` it checks `if (this._refreshPromise) { ... return await this._refreshPromise }` ("Token refresh already in progress, waiting for completion") before starting a new refresh, and `retrieveAuthInfo()` awaits the same promise. This coalesces concurrent token-refresh network calls into one.

The same coalescing pattern appears elsewhere if you meant a different request:
- `src/core/controller/models/refreshClineRecommendedModels.ts` — module-level `pendingRefresh`, returned directly if non-null in `refreshClineRecommendedModels()`.
- `src/services/banner/BannerService.ts` — `fetchPromise` guarded by `!this.fetchPromise` in `ensureFreshCache()`.
- `src/services/auth/oca/OcaAuthService.ts` — `_refreshInFlight`.

If you have a specific request in mind (auth token, model list, banners), tell me which and I'll point to the exact guard.

The in-flight request dedup (preventing a second identical network call while one is already running) is the token-refresh concurrency guard.

**Primary:** `src/services/auth/AuthService.ts` — in `internalGetAuthToken()`, the `_refreshPromise` field. If a refresh is already in progress, callers `await this._refreshPromise` instead of firing a second refresh; it's set to `null` in `finally`.

The same pattern also guards Codex token refresh: `src/integrations/openai-codex/oauth.ts` — `refreshPromise` (`if (!this.refreshPromise) { this.refreshPromise = refreshAccessToken(...) }`).

The XML-like streamed text is parsed into structured tool actions in:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`**

It scans the raw assistant string for tool/param XML tags and emits an array of `AssistantMessageContent` (`TextStreamContent` | `ToolUse` | `ReasoningStreamContent`), marking unclosed trailing blocks as `partial`. It's re-exported from `src/core/assistant-message/index.ts`.

(Note: this is the XML-tool path. Native/function-call tool outputs are handled separately by the API handlers rather than this text parser.)

The streamed assistant text is parsed into structured tool actions by **`parseAssistantMessageV2`** in **`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** (re-exported from `src/core/assistant-message/index.ts`).

It scans the raw model output for XML-like tool/parameter tags and builds an array of `ToolUse` objects (with `name` and `params`), marking unclosed blocks as `partial` for streaming.

This is the request-coalescing (single-flight) pattern you're describing.

**File:** `src/services/auth/AuthService.ts`
**Symbol:** `internalGetAuthToken()` — guarded by the `_refreshPromise` field

When an auth-token refresh is needed, it stores the in-flight refresh in `this._refreshPromise`. Any concurrent caller that finds `_refreshPromise` already set just `await`s the existing promise instead of firing a second network refresh (line 166-170), and the field is reset to `null` in the `finally` block once the request completes.

The OCA auth service uses the same pattern via `_refreshInFlight` in `src/services/auth/oca/OcaAuthService.ts`.

The mechanism is in‑flight request coalescing via a module‑level `pendingRefresh` promise guard.

**File:** `src/core/controller/models/refreshOpenRouterModels.ts`
**Symbol:** the `pendingRefresh` promise in `refreshOpenRouterModels()` — if a fetch is already running it returns that same promise instead of starting a second one (lines 80–110).

The same pattern is repeated for the other model providers: `refreshBasetenModels.ts`, `refreshGroqModels.ts`, `refreshClineModels.ts`, and `refreshVercelAiGatewayModels.ts` (each with its own `pendingRefresh`).

If you meant a different network call, other places guard the same way: `BannerService.getActiveBanners()` (`this.fetchPromise` in `src/services/banner/BannerService.ts`) and `AuthService` token refresh (`this._refreshPromise`).

**File:** `src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts` — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`** (re-exported from `src/core/assistant-message/index.ts`).

It parses the model's raw streamed text, scanning for XML-like tool/param tags, into an array of structured `AssistantMessageContent` blocks (`TextStreamContent` / `ToolUse` / `ReasoningStreamContent`). The resulting `ToolUse` objects are what the rest of the system executes as tool actions.

(Note: native tool calls — Responses API providers — bypass this XML parsing and arrive already structured.)

The codebase guards against duplicate concurrent network requests in a few places, but the canonical "don't fire the same request twice at once" pattern is the **`pendingRefresh` module-level promise** in the model-refresh functions.

**File / symbol:** `pendingRefresh` in `src/core/controller/models/refreshOpenRouterModels.ts:81` (and the function `refreshOpenRouterModels`, lines 95–98 return the in-flight promise instead of starting a new fetch).

The identical pattern is repeated in the sibling refreshers — `refreshClineModels.ts`, `refreshBasetenModels.ts`, `refreshGroqModels.ts`, `refreshHicapModels.ts`, and `refreshVercelAiGatewayModels.ts`.

A related (token-refresh) variant is `OpenAiCodexOAuthManager.refreshPromise` in `src/integrations/openai-codex/oauth.ts:361` ("De-dupe concurrent refreshes").

The streamed text is parsed in:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`** (re-exported from `src/core/assistant-message/index.ts`).

It incrementally scans the model's raw streamed output and turns it into structured `AssistantMessageContent[]` — `TextStreamContent`, `ReasoningStreamContent`, and `ToolUse` blocks (each `ToolUse` carrying the tool `name` and parsed `params`), which then drive execution downstream.

(Note: this is the XML-style tool parser; providers using native tool calls produce `ToolUse` objects directly with `isNativeToolCall: true` rather than going through this text parser.)

For XML-style tool calls, the parsing happens in:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`**

This is the streaming parser that scans the model's accumulated text output and segments it into `AssistantMessageContent[]` — `TextStreamContent`, `ReasoningStreamContent`, and `ToolUse` blocks (the structured tool actions, with `name` and `params`). It's re-exported from `src/core/assistant-message/index.ts`.

(Native/Responses-API tool calls bypass this XML parser and arrive as structured `ToolUse` objects directly with `isNativeToolCall: true`.)

The streamed assistant text is parsed into structured tool actions in:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`**

It scans the raw model output for XML-like tags, building an array of `AssistantMessageContent` objects (`TextContent` and `ToolUse` blocks, with `partial: true` for blocks still streaming). The types it produces are defined in `src/core/assistant-message/index.ts`.

Note this is the XML-tool path; providers using OpenAI's Responses API use native tool calling instead of this parser.

The streamed assistant text is parsed into structured tool actions in:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`**

It scans the raw streamed string for XML-like tags (`<tool_name>`, `<param>`) and produces an array of `AssistantMessageContent` blocks (`TextContent` / `ToolUse` with `params`), marking incomplete blocks as `partial` for streaming.

(Note: this is the XML-tool path. Providers using OpenAI's Responses API use native tool calling instead, where tool calls arrive pre-structured rather than being parsed from text.)

The streamed assistant text is parsed into structured tool actions in:

**`src/core/assistant-message/parse-assistant-message.ts`** — function **`parseAssistantMessageV2`**

It scans the raw model output for XML-like tags, emitting an array of `AssistantMessageContent` blocks (`text` and `tool_use`), extracting tool names and their parameters, and marking blocks `partial` when the stream ends mid-tag. (This handles XML-style tools; providers using native tool calling bypass this path.)

The chunk-streaming loop checks for interruption after each chunk, so a long-running API request gets broken out partway through there.

**`src/core/task/index.ts`** — inside the `attemptApiRequest`/stream-consuming loop, at the `while (true)` over `streamCoordinator.nextChunk()`. After processing each chunk it checks `this.taskState.abort` (line ~2961), calls `this.api.abort?.()` + `abortStream("user_cancelled")`, and `break`s out of the stream (`shouldInterruptStream = true`). Related break-points in the same loop: `this.taskState.didRejectTool` (tool rejection, ~2971) and the parallel-tool-disabled interrupt just below.

The abort flag itself is set by `abortTask()` (line ~1534) in the same file.

**`src/core/task/index.ts` → `recursivelyMakeClineRequests()`** (the `while (true)` stream-consumption loop, around line 2961).

As each chunk arrives from the model stream, the loop checks `this.taskState.abort`; when set, it calls `this.api.abort?.()`, runs `abortStream("user_cancelled")`, and `break`s out — interrupting the in-flight request mid-stream. The stream itself comes from `attemptApiRequest()` (line 1865), and the abort flag is flipped by `abortTask()` (line 1534).

The loop also breaks early for non-cancellation reasons in the same spot: `didRejectTool` (user feedback interrupts the response) and the parallel-tool-calling guard just below.

A long-running API request (the streaming LLM response) is interrupted partway through in:

**`src/core/task/index.ts` → `recursivelyMakeClineRequests()`**

Specifically inside the `for await (const chunk of stream)` loop (stream comes from `attemptApiRequest`). After each chunk it checks `this.taskState.abort`; when set, it calls `this.api.abort?.()`, runs the local `abortStream("user_cancelled")`, and `break`s out of the stream mid-response (`src/core/task/index.ts:2961`).

The abort flag itself is flipped by `abortTask()` (same file, `src/core/task/index.ts:1534`), which is what makes the running stream stop at the next chunk boundary.

The long-running request — the streaming API response — can be interrupted partway through inside its chunk-consumption loop.

**File:** `src/core/task/index.ts`
**Function:** `recursivelyMakeClineRequests` (line 2354)

The interruption happens in the `while (true)` stream-reading loop (starting line 2858). After each chunk is processed, the loop checks three break conditions:

- **User cancellation** (`index.ts:2961`): `if (this.taskState.abort)` → calls `this.api.abort?.()`, runs `abortStream("user_cancelled")`, and breaks.
- **Tool rejection** (`index.ts:2971`): `if (this.taskState.didRejectTool)` → appends `[Response interrupted by user feedback]` and breaks.
- **Single-tool-use interrupt** (`index.ts:2982`): when parallel tool calling is disabled and a tool was already used, appends `[Response interrupted by a tool use result...]` and breaks.

When any fire, `shouldInterruptStream` is set and `streamCoordinator.stop()` (line 2991) tears down the in-flight stream instead of letting it complete.

The streaming consumption loop lives in `recursivelyMakeClineRequests`, and the per-chunk abort check is the interruption point.

**File:** `src/core/task/index.ts`
**Function:** `recursivelyMakeClineRequests` — inside its `for await (const chunk of stream)` loop, the abort check at **lines 2961–2969**:

```ts
if (this.taskState.abort) {
    this.api.abort?.()
    if (!this.taskState.abandoned) {
        await abortStream("user_cancelled")
    }
    shouldInterruptStream = true
    break // aborts the stream
}
```

After each chunk from `attemptApiRequest`, the loop checks `this.taskState.abort`; if set, it calls `this.api.abort?.()` and the local `abortStream(...)` helper, then breaks out — interrupting the long-running API request partway through. (Two adjacent checks at lines 2971 and 2982 similarly break the loop early on tool rejection / single-tool-use, via `shouldInterruptStream`.)

The interruption point is the streaming loop inside `src/core/task/index.ts`, in the `recursivelyMakeClineRequests` method (the `while (true)` loop over `streamCoordinator.nextChunk()`, starting ~line 2858).

Mid-stream, after each chunk it checks three conditions and sets `shouldInterruptStream = true` to break out and call `streamCoordinator.stop()`:

- **`this.taskState.abort`** (user cancelled) — line 2961, also calls `this.api.abort?.()` and `abortStream("user_cancelled")`
- **`this.taskState.didRejectTool`** (user rejected a tool) — line 2971
- **`!isParallelToolCallingEnabled() && this.taskState.didAlreadyUseTool`** (one tool per message) — line 2982

So: **`src/core/task/index.ts` → `recursivelyMakeClineRequests` (the chunk loop driven by `shouldInterruptStream`).**

The long-running request (the LLM API stream) is interrupted inside the streaming loop of `recursivelyMakeClineRequests`.

**File:** `src/core/task/index.ts`
**Function/symbol:** `Task.recursivelyMakeClineRequests` — specifically the `for await (...)` loop over the API stream, where each chunk checks `this.taskState.abort` (around `index.ts:2961`) and, if set, calls `this.api.abort?.()` + `abortStream("user_cancelled")` and `break`s out of the stream.

The abort flag itself is flipped by `Task.abortTask()` (`index.ts:1534`, sets `this.taskState.abort = true`), and the per-chunk check is what lets a long-running request stop partway through.

The long-running streaming API request is interrupted in:

**`src/core/task/index.ts` — `recursivelyMakeClineRequests()`**

Inside the `while (true)` chunk-consumption loop (`streamCoordinator.nextChunk()`), each iteration checks the abort flag at **line 2961**:

```ts
if (this.taskState.abort) {
    this.api.abort?.()
    if (!this.taskState.abandoned) {
        await abortStream("user_cancelled")   // graceful abort
    }
    shouldInterruptStream = true
    break  // aborts the stream
}
```

So the interruption point is between chunks of the streamed response — the loop checks `this.taskState.abort` (set by `abortTask()`, line 1534) after processing each chunk and bails out via `this.api.abort()` + the local `abortStream` helper (defined at line 2723). There's also a sibling check for tool rejection (`didRejectTool`, line 2971) that interrupts the response mid-stream with `[Response interrupted by user feedback]`.

A long-running streaming request is interrupted in:

**File:** `src/core/task/index.ts`
**Function/symbol:** `recursivelyMakeClineRequests()` — inside its chunk-consumption `while (true)` loop, the abort check at ~line 2961:

```typescript
if (this.taskState.abort) {
    this.api.abort?.()
    if (!this.taskState.abandoned) {
        await abortStream("user_cancelled")
    }
    shouldInterruptStream = true
    break
}
```

This is the primary cut point: between consuming stream chunks, it checks `taskState.abort` (set by `abortTask()`), signals the API handler via `this.api.abort?.()`, saves the partial response with `abortStream("user_cancelled")`, and breaks out — interrupting the request partway through.

The code that strips internal/private fields from messages before they go out to an LLM provider lives here:

**`src/shared/messages/content.ts`** — `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` (with its helper `cleanContentBlock()`)

These convert a stored `ClineStorageMessage` into a plain `Anthropic.MessageParam`, removing Cline-internal/private fields (`modelInfo`, `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and Gemini-only `signature`) and dropping invalid thinking blocks. The intent is documented right on the type:

```ts
// src/shared/messages/content.ts:90-95
/**
 * NOTE: model information used when generating this message.
 * Internal use for message conversion only.
 * MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider.
 */
modelInfo?: ClineMessageModelInfo
```

Note `reasoning_details` are kept only for providers in `REASONING_DETAILS_PROVIDERS` (`["cline", "openrouter"]`) and stripped for everyone else.

`src/shared/messages/content.ts` — the function **`convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()`** (with its helper **`cleanContentBlock()`**).

It strips Cline-internal/private fields (e.g. `modelInfo`, `metrics`, `reasoning_details`) and invalid thinking blocks off each stored message when converting `ClineStorageMessage` → Anthropic `MessageParam`. The intent is stated directly at `content.ts:90-93`: *"Internal use for message conversion only. MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider."*

(Note: there are also provider-specific strippers, e.g. `stripThinkingTags` in `src/core/api/providers/cerebras.ts:71`, which removes `<think>…</think>` from history for reasoning models — but the central, all-provider stripping happens in `content.ts`.)

**`src/shared/messages/content.ts`** — `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()`, with its helper `cleanContentBlock()`.

These strip Cline-internal/private fields off each stored message right before it's handed to an LLM provider. `cleanContentBlock()` removes `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and the Gemini-only `signature`; `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` also drops `modelInfo`/`metrics` and filters out invalid (signature-less) thinking blocks. The intent is documented inline on `ClineStorageMessage.modelInfo` (line 93): *"MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider."*

(Separately, `src/core/task/index.ts` in `presentAssistantMessage` strips `<thinking>`/`<think>`/`<function_calls>` tags — but that's for hiding content in the chat UI, not for outbound API requests.)

**`src/shared/messages/content.ts`** — `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()`, which delegates to **`cleanContentBlock()`**.

`cleanContentBlock()` strips the Cline-internal/private fields (`reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and the Gemini-only `signature`) off each content block, and `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` also drops the message-level `modelInfo`/`metrics` and filters out signature-less thinking blocks — so only Anthropic-compatible content goes out to the LLM provider. The intent is spelled out in the `ClineStorageMessage.modelInfo` doc comment: *"MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider."*

(If you instead meant image-stripping for Claude Code, that's `filterMessagesForClaudeCode()` in `src/integrations/claude-code/message-filter.ts` — but that hides images, not private fields.)

**File:** `src/shared/messages/content.ts`

**Function/symbol:** `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` (and its helper `cleanContentBlock()`)

This is where Cline strips its internal/private message fields before sending to an LLM provider. The `ClineStorageMessage` type carries Cline-only metadata (`modelInfo`, `metrics`, `ts`, plus per-block `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and Gemini `signature`) — the comment on `modelInfo` (line 92–94) notes it "MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider."

- `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` drops those fields and also filters out invalid `thinking` blocks lacking a signature.
- `cleanContentBlock()` does the per-block removal of the Cline-specific keys.

Note: this strips Cline's internal bookkeeping, not user-secret redaction. If you instead meant `.clineignore` file-content gating, that lives in `src/core/ignore/ClineIgnoreController.ts` — let me know which sense of "private" you're after.

`src/shared/messages/content.ts` — the function **`convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage`**, with its helper **`cleanContentBlock`**.

These strip Cline-internal/private fields from each stored message before it's sent to an LLM provider — `modelInfo` (explicitly flagged at `content.ts:90-93`: "MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider"), plus `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, Gemini-only `signature`s, and invalid (unsigned) thinking blocks. It's invoked from `sanitizeAnthropicMessages` in `src/core/api/transform/anthropic-format.ts` on the way out.

**File:** `src/shared/messages/content.ts`

**Function:** `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` (with its helper `cleanContentBlock()`)

This is where Cline strips its internal/"private" fields from stored messages before they go to an LLM provider. The `ClineStorageMessage` type carries Cline-only metadata — `modelInfo` (commented `MUST be removed before sending message to any LLM provider`), `metrics`, `ts`, plus per-block fields like `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and the Gemini-only `signature`. `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage` filters out invalid thinking blocks (no signature) and maps each block through `cleanContentBlock`, which deletes those extra fields so only Anthropic-compatible content is sent (skipped for providers in `REASONING_DETAILS_PROVIDERS`).

**`src/shared/messages/content.ts` → `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()`** (with its helper `cleanContentBlock()`).

It takes Cline's internal `ClineStorageMessage` and strips the Cline-only/private fields before the message goes to the API — keeping only `{ role, content }` (dropping `modelInfo`, `metrics`, `id`, `ts`) and removing per-block fields like `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and stray `signature`. It also drops invalid thinking blocks (no signature).

It's invoked via `sanitizeAnthropicMessages()` in `src/core/api/transform/anthropic-format.ts`, which runs before each request for Anthropic-format providers.

Note: there's no `<private>`-tag redaction of user text — "private" here means Cline-internal metadata, not user-marked secrets.

## Answer

**File:** `src/shared/messages/content.ts`
**Function:** `cleanContentBlock()` (and its caller `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()`)

`cleanContentBlock()` (`src/shared/messages/content.ts:137`) strips Cline-internal/"private" fields off each message content block — `reasoning_details`, `call_id`, `summary`, and the Gemini-only `signature` on non-thinking blocks — returning only Anthropic-API-compatible fields.

It's invoked by `convertClineStorageToAnthropicMessage()` (`content.ts:111`), which converts stored messages to the outbound API format right before they're sent to the LLM — dropping internal fields and also filtering out invalid (unsigned) thinking blocks. That converter is driven in batch by `sanitizeAnthropicMessages()` in `src/core/api/transform/anthropic-format.ts:14`, used by the Anthropic/Vertex/AIHubmix/OCA provider paths.

Note: this strips Cline's *internal bookkeeping* fields, not user-authored "secret" text. If by "private content" you meant something else (e.g., redacting secrets/API keys for telemetry, or hiding content in the webview), let me know and I'll point you to that path instead.
